takeaway: Jules Walter's guest essay is the canonical definition: product sense is "the skill of consistently being able to craft products (or make changes to existing products) that have the intended impact on their users," and — the load-bearing claim for the whole topic — "product sense is not something you need to be born with. It's a learned skill, just like any other PM skill." He grounds it in two ingredients, empathy "to discover meaningful user needs" and creativity "to come up with solutions that effectively address those needs," and prescribes concrete reps: observe users in research 2–4 times monthly, and deconstruct products 1–2 hours monthly by "examining new applications, comparing competing offerings, and analyzing your own reactions." The progress signal is that you start to "notice subtle things about products and people that you would have missed before."

Nikkel Blaase widens Walter's definition to include the business: "Good Product Sense is the acquired ability to design products or changes to existing products in a way that has the intended effect on their users or customers and also has a positive effect on the business itself." He gives the four-factor decomposition most other writers echo — "empathy to discover meaningful user needs, product and domain knowledge of how successful solutions in the market work, creative problem-solving to effectively meet user and business needs alike, and finally, experience gained over time in product development" — and is explicit on the central debate: "this is not an innate ability, but something that can be learned and developed over time."

Julie Zhuo's a16z/Future essay frames the adjacent skill, product thinking — "The skill of knowing what makes a product useful — and loved — by people" — and names its two habits: observation, "paying attention to people's reactions when they encounter products or services in their day-to-day lives," and inquiry, "asking the 'why' behind your observations." Her stance on the innate-vs-learned question is unambiguous: "As with all skills, it can be nurtured and developed; it's not just an instinct one does or doesn't have," and "Excelling at product thinking is not innate; growth comes from practice." Her prescribed cadence is concrete: "Every week, try at least one new product, feature, or service" and "have at least one conversation or reflection about how a specific product decision impacts its intended audience."

Zhuo's product-critique piece operationalizes how to build sense by close observation: a critique is "exploring the curiosity of why some products and experiences work for people, and why others don't," and she insists "Product design isn't some innate skill one is born with, like having a good ear for distinguishing notes or possessing exceptionally powerful twitch muscles." Her structured questions — "What's the experience of getting started or signing up?", "How does this app explain itself in the first minute?", "How did you feel while exploring the app?" — turn a vague gut reaction into examinable evidence, anchored by the observation that "Most opinions of a product are formed within the first few minutes." The closing discipline: "There is no shortcut to developing better product instincts besides keen and close observation."

Rohan Sandeep gives a reusable critique scaffold built on Zhuo's three-stage frame — "before, first time usage, and weeks after" — and four interface questions a user should be able to answer without technical baggage: "Where am I, Why Am I here, What can I do, and how can I do it." He links the practice to building general judgment: examining "Doors, … switches, … Instagram, … ERP applications, … browsers" develops "a larger sense of what a product does, and how it does it," and he recommends comparative deconstruction ("How does Snapchat compare with Instagram, or how does chrome compare with Safari") plus the classic cognitive-walkthrough method. Critique is the lab where pattern-recognition gets reps.

Sam Polgar's synthesis lands the consensus line — "Product Sense is a learned ability — not an unattainable, god-given gift" — and supplies a repeatable before/during/after deconstruction loop: before use, "identify marketed value, desired outcomes, and reference sources"; during, "assess onboarding, intuitiveness, features, comparisons, engagement, thoughts, and feelings"; after, "evaluate outcome achievement, alignment between marketing and experience, doubts, and improvement opportunities." By "consistently asking the same questions about different products, practitioners develop pattern recognition." He adds an underrated caveat — "your mental and emotional state will affect your Product Sense" — so the reps only pay off when done with "an inquisitive and empathetic mind" and the "ability to listen without bias or prejudice."

Academy of PM frames sense as critical-plus-market thinking — "Product sense is thinking creatively and critically about a product's strengths and weaknesses" and "looking beyond your opinion and preference about a product to anticipate how the broader market will react" — and rejects mystique outright: "Product sense is not magic. It's not a sixth sense that comes out of thin air. It's a skill that you can learn," likening it to cooking. Its three development methods are "Observation, Curiosity, [and] Repetition" ("Getting great at anything takes practice. Do the work"), with a five-question drill: what's great, what's not, what's the company's goal and target market, did they nail it, and how would I make it better.

This Mind the Product essay defines the sibling concept, taste, as "the ability to see beyond what is simply functional or trendy. It's about having an instinct for what feels right," combining "insights from data and user feedback with a vision that is aesthetically pleasing, intuitive, and deeply aligned with users' needs." It is unapologetic that craft is the tell — "that product was built by a team that cared deeply about the details" — and that "great design is not just a discipline or a process—it's an intuition." On whether taste can be grown, it leans organizational: "hiring and nurturing people with a strong sense of product design intuition" and creating "an environment where this kind of thinking is valued," via rituals like a "Why can't we have nice things?" workshop to "raise the bar."

Marty Cagan supplies the craft-and-judgment anchor: "Our job as product managers is to be stubborn on vision, but flexible on details" — the posture that lets taste operate without becoming dogma. His humility check on raw intuition is the line "Most of your ideas suck," with the corollary that teams should "kill at least half their ideas" and "test" rather than argue ("In a good product culture, you don't have endless meetings, you don't have a bunch of escalations; you test"). And he frames where instinct comes from organizationally — "If you're just using your developers to code, you're only getting half their value" — locating product judgment in a team that confronts real customer problems, not in a lone visionary.

Exponent's guide reveals what "good product sense" looks like when it's being graded, naming five signals interviewers score: user empathy ("Do you genuinely think from the user's perspective, or do you jump straight to solutions?"), structured thinking ("Can you break an ambiguous problem into a clear, navigable process?"), product taste ("Are your ideas interesting, specific, and grounded in real user value, or generic?"), strategic awareness, and communication. The bar has risen: "a structured answer used to be a reasonable signal. Now it is a baseline," so execution quality, not a memorized framework, separates candidates. One staff PM's priority order was breaking ambiguity into structure fast, organized communication, and digging "into personas with real detail."

Product School is the useful counter-voice on the innate question — it calls product sense "the innate ability to understand what makes a product great and how it can meet the market's needs," language most practitioner-canon explicitly rejects, which is why the debate is live rather than settled. Its practical contribution is the framework menu interviewers see candidates lean on: CIRCLES ("Comprehend, Identify, Report, Customers, List solutions, Evaluate trade-offs, Summarize"), STAR, and BUS ("Business objectives, User problems, Solutions"). What it says interviewers actually want is constant across sources: "a candidate's ability to empathize with users and to innovate within market constraints."

ProdPad reframes the interview as a window onto a career-long skill: "Product sense isn't something that you should focus on just to master the product sense interview. No, this is a skill that you'll want to develop your entire career." On evaluation it stresses reasoning over answers — "The whole point of this interview is to see your reasoning, so make sure you're prepared to go over every step of your decision-making process" — and enumerates what good looks like: "thinking about and being empathetic to users, … strong problem definition, … intuitive design standards, and that you can think strategically." The best evidence is lived: "tie their answers back to real-world experiences, showcasing how their product sense has led them to make effective decisions in past roles."

Yale's career-development guide treats sense as buildable — "the ability to design products that address user needs" that "combines empathy, domain knowledge, and creativity" — with prescriptions to "talk to different types of users and partners," study "cognitive biases, including behavioral economics," and deconstruct your domain's apps ("why this button was put in this place, or why this animation was done in that manner"). It is also a candid dissenting note on one ingredient: "You can't teach creativity; it's one of those things that you're mostly born with. It will be challenging if you aren't naturally gifted here, but it isn't impossible" — a useful hedge that even pro-learnable sources concede creativity may be the partially-innate component.

This piece carries the framework-skeptic camp: "Landing a product manager role requires more than just reciting frameworks," and tools "like the CIRCLES Method or the AARRR Framework are valuable tools, but they should be seen as starting points rather than rigid blueprints" because "Real-world product challenges rarely fit neatly into predefined boxes." Real product sense is "the ability to understand users, identify opportunities, and envision solutions that create value," cultivated through user empathy, analytical skill, and strategic thinking — not memorization. Its honest closing: "Cultivating a strong product sense takes time and dedicated effort. It's a journey of continuous learning and improvement."

Wikipedia anchors the provenance of the most-cited product-sense voice: Julie Zhuo "was the vice president of product design at Facebook and now co-founder at Sundial," having "joined Facebook in May 2006 as its first intern and became a manager at age 25," and authored "The Making of a Manager: What to Do When Everyone Looks to You" (2019). The biographical grounding matters because so much product-sense canon traces to her writing on design intuition — citing the practitioner's actual track record is part of the trust discipline.
